htmlContent:
The spine remembers what the mind forgets

I spent years thinking my lower back was just broken. Structurally defective. A design flaw in my particular model of human. I did the PT, the stretches, the foam rolling, the core work. I saw chiropractors who cracked me like a knuckle. I saw massage therapists who dug elbows into my psoas until I saw stars. And every time, the pain came back within days. Sometimes hours.

Here's the thing. My back wasn't the problem. It was just the messenger.

I remember sitting in my car after a particularly brutal flare up, unable to turn my head to check my blind spot. And I started crying. Not from the pain, though there was plenty of that. I was crying because I was so tired. Tired of fighting my own body. Tired of the endless search for the one fix that would finally make it stop. Tired of feeling like a broken machine.

It took me years to understand what was actually happening. And I want to save you some of that time if I can.

Because chronic back pain and early attachment disruption are not just correlated. They are, in many cases, the same story told in two different languages. One language is words and memories. The other is tissue and nerve.

What attachment has to do with your lumbar spine

Let me back up a bit. John Bowlby, the British psychiatrist who first developed attachment theory, spent decades watching what happens to infants when their caregivers are inconsistent, absent, or threatening. He saw that babies develop strategies to survive those environments. Some become hypervigilant, constantly scanning for danger. Others shut down, dissociate, go numb. Some oscillate wildly between reaching out and pulling away.

These are not choices. They are biological adaptations. And they don't just live in your brain. They live in your body.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown that trauma and attachment disruption get encoded in the physical structure of the nervous system. Your muscles remember. Your fascia remembers. Your autonomic nervous system remembers. And your back, specifically, is a prime real estate location for this kind of storage.

Think about it. The muscles along your spine are densely packed with nerve endings. They are directly connected to the sympathetic chain ganglia, which is the highway your fight-or-flight response travels on. When your nervous system is constantly on alert because it learned early on that safety was unreliable, those muscles don't fully relax. Ever. They hold a low-grade tension, waiting for the threat. Days turn into months. Months turn into years. And eventually, that constant low-grade tension becomes chronic pain.

I can feel my own lower back tighten as I write this. Notice what just happened in your body. Did something shift when you read that? A twinge? A clench? A familiar ache that you've been ignoring?

That's not a coincidence.

The psoas: the muscle that holds your history

There is a specific muscle group that deserves its own spotlight here. The psoas (pronounced so-as) is a deep core muscle that connects your lumbar spine to your femur. It's the only muscle in your body that connects your upper half to your lower half. And it is exquisitely sensitive to emotional state.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about the psoas as a "muscle of the soul." When you're frightened, the psoas contracts to prepare you to run or fight. When you're safe, it softens and allows your pelvis to move freely. But if you grew up in an environment where safety was never guaranteed, your psoas may have learned to stay contracted. Permanently.

I once had a bodyworker put her hand on my psoas and ask me what I was feeling. I said "nothing." She waited. Then I started sobbing. Not sad sobbing. Primal sobbing. The kind that comes from somewhere deeper than memory. The kind that is pure nervous system release.

That muscle was holding a story my conscious mind had no access to. But my body knew. It always knew.

Chronic back pain from attachment disruption often shows up as a specific pattern. It's not sharp or acute. It's dull, aching, constant. It shifts around but never fully leaves. It gets worse under stress. It gets worse when you're trying to rest. It gets worse when you're in relationships that trigger your old attachment wounds.

Sound familiar?

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is the TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). I use mine not to "fix" my back, but to gently remind my nervous system that it's okay to let go. Slow, breath-focused rolling. Not aggressive. Just present.

Why the wellness industry gets this so wrong

Here's where I need to be direct with you. The wellness industry has failed people with chronic back pain. Spectacularly. They sell you the idea that if you just do the right stretch, buy the right pillow, get the right alignment, the pain will go away. And when it doesn't, they imply that you're not trying hard enough.

That's not just unhelpful. It's retraumatizing.

Because if your pain is rooted in attachment disruption, then treating it as a purely mechanical problem is like trying to fix a leak in your roof by mopping the floor. You're addressing the symptom, not the source. And you're exhausting yourself in the process.

I've seen people spend thousands on inversion tables, special mattresses, expensive supplements, and elaborate stretching routines. And they still hurt. Because nobody asked them the real question: What happened to you? Not what happened to your spine. What happened to you.

Janina Fisher, a leading expert in trauma treatment, talks about how the body holds "survival responses" that don't know the threat is over. Your nervous system is still running the program it installed in childhood. And that program includes specific patterns of muscle tension, breathing restriction, and postural bracing.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate is well-absorbed and can help relax muscles and support the nervous system. It's not a cure. But it can take the edge off while you do the deeper work.

What actually helps: working with the nervous system, not against it

So what do you do if you suspect your chronic back pain is connected to attachment disruption? You don't start with stretching. You start with safety.

Your nervous system needs to learn, at a somatic level, that it is safe enough to soften. And that takes time. It takes repetition. And it takes a completely different approach than what most back pain protocols offer.

Here are some things that have helped me and the people I work with:

First, orienting. This is a technique from Somatic Experiencing. You slowly look around your environment and notice things that are neutral or pleasant. The color of the wall. The shape of a leaf. The way light falls on a surface. This tells your nervous system, "I am here, in this moment, and I am not in danger." Do it for a few minutes before you try any physical intervention.

Second, pendulation. This is the practice of moving between a place of tension and a place of ease in your body. You notice the tightness in your back. You acknowledge it. Then you move your attention to somewhere that feels neutral or pleasant. Your left hand. Your right foot. The sensation of your breath moving through your nostrils. You go back and forth. Over time, the nervous system learns that it can visit the pain without being consumed by it.

Third, breath. Specifically, long, slow exhales. The exhale is the relaxation phase of the breath cycle. It activates the vagus nerve and signals the parasympathetic nervous system to come online. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is the Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It creates gentle resistance on the exhale, which naturally lengthens it. I use mine when I feel the familiar clench starting in my lower back.

Fourth, noise reduction. If your nervous system is hypervigilant from early attachment disruption, it's constantly processing sensory input. Sound is a big one. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower ambient noise without blocking it completely. I wear them sometimes just to give my nervous system a break from having to filter everything.

And fifth, finding a practitioner who understands this stuff. A bodyworker, therapist, or Somatic Experiencing practitioner who gets that your back pain is not just mechanical. Someone who will work with your nervous system, not just your muscles.

I'm not saying you'll never need another adjustment or another stretch session. I'm saying that if you only work at the level of mechanics, you'll miss the deeper layer. And that deeper layer is where the real change happens.

Stay with me here. This is not a quick fix. This is not a 30-day challenge. This is a renegotiation of your relationship with your own body. And that takes as long as it takes.
